mechanism_narrative: The federal funds rate at 2.56% combined with rising core inflation (CPIAUCSL at 296.761) signals monetary policy is restrictive relative to inflation dynamics. This real rate compression forces investors to demand higher yields on long-duration assets like 10-year Treasuries to maintain real return expectations, driving yields upward over the next 20 trading days.
evidence_refs: fact:Real federal funds rate (2.56% - inflation) is negative or near-zero in April 2008
failure_condition: 10-year Treasury yield declines over 20-day forward window despite negative real rates, or Rank IC of FEDFUNDS-CPIAUCSL interaction falls below 0.05 in rolling 1-year windows during periods of negative real rates.

mechanism_narrative: Unemployment rate at 3.5% is near cyclical lows, indicating tight labor market conditions. Tight labor markets historically precede wage acceleration and inflation surprises, which force the Federal Reserve to maintain or raise policy rates, pushing 10-year Treasury yields higher as market participants price in sustained monetary restriction.
evidence_refs: fact:UNRATE at 3.5% in April 2008 is at or near cycle lows
regime_hint: late-cycle expansion with tight labor markets
failure_condition: 10-year Treasury yields fall by more than 25 basis points over the 60-day forward window despite UNRATE remaining below 4.0%, or wage growth (if observable) does not accelerate within 3 months of tight labor market signal.

mechanism_narrative: Retail sales (RSAFS at 683,974 million) and housing starts (HOUST at 1,575 thousand) are both declining from prior peaks, signaling demand weakness. Demand contraction reduces inflation expectations and increases recession risk, prompting investors to rotate into safe-haven long-duration assets, driving 10-year Treasury yields downward over the next 20 trading days.
candidate_trigger: RSAFS declines month-over-month or HOUST falls below 1,600 thousand units
evidence_refs: fact:Housing starts declining sharply in early 2008 as subprime crisis unfolds
regime_hint: demand shock and early recession signal
failure_condition: 10-year Treasury yields rise by more than 20 basis points over 20-day window despite concurrent RSAFS and HOUST declines, or correlation between demand weakness and yield direction is negative (Rank IC < -0.10) in rolling 3-month windows.

mechanism_narrative: Industrial production (INDPRO at 104.5464) is flat to declining, indicating supply-side weakness. Supply contraction without corresponding demand destruction creates stagflationary pressure, forcing the Fed to maintain restrictive policy despite growth concerns. This policy-rate floor pushes 10-year Treasury yields higher as term premium expands.
candidate_trigger: INDPRO declines month-over-month for two consecutive months
evidence_refs: fact:Industrial production weakness in early 2008 amid energy price shocks
failure_condition: 10-year Treasury yields fall by more than 15 basis points over 5-day window despite INDPRO decline, or INDPRO declines do not precede yield increases within 10 trading days in more than 40% of triggered instances.

mechanism_narrative: Payroll employment (PAYEMS at 153,018 thousand) is beginning to decelerate from prior growth rates. Employment deceleration signals weakening labor demand and rising recession risk, which compresses long-term growth expectations and reduces inflation expectations, driving 10-year Treasury yields downward as investors price in lower future policy rates.
candidate_trigger: PAYEMS month-over-month growth falls below 50 thousand
evidence_refs: fact:Payroll growth slowing in early 2008 as financial crisis impacts labor demand
failure_condition: 10-year Treasury yields rise by more than 25 basis points over 20-day window despite PAYEMS growth deceleration, or Rank IC of PAYEMS growth rate falls below 0.04 in rolling 6-month windows during periods of employment deceleration.

mechanism_narrative: Real GDP (GDPC1 at 19,895.271 billion chained 2017 dollars) growth is decelerating from trend. GDP deceleration combined with elevated inflation (CPIAUCSL) creates policy uncertainty: the Fed faces a dilemma between supporting growth and fighting inflation. This policy uncertainty increases term premium on long-duration assets, pushing 10-year Treasury yields higher.
candidate_trigger: GDPC1 quarter-over-quarter growth falls below 2.0% annualized
evidence_refs: fact:Q1 2008 GDP growth slowing amid financial turmoil and persistent inflation
failure_condition: 10-year Treasury yields fall by more than 30 basis points over 60-day window despite GDP deceleration and elevated inflation, or term premium (measured as 10Y-2Y spread) contracts when GDP growth slows in more than 50% of instances.

mechanism_narrative: The federal funds rate at 2.56% is already at restrictive levels relative to neutral rate estimates (approximately 2.5-3.0%). Further rate cuts are unlikely given inflation concerns, creating a floor on policy rates. This policy rate floor prevents yields from falling as much as growth weakness would otherwise suggest, keeping 10-year Treasury yields elevated relative to fundamentals.
candidate_trigger: FEDFUNDS remains above 2.25% for two consecutive months
evidence_refs: fact:Fed funds rate at 2.56% in April 2008, near estimated neutral rate
regime_hint: monetary policy constraint at lower bound
failure_condition: 10-year Treasury yields fall by more than 40 basis points over 60-day window despite FEDFUNDS remaining above 2.25%, or policy rate floor does not prevent yield compression in more than 45% of instances when growth weakens.

mechanism_narrative: Housing starts (HOUST at 1,575 thousand) are collapsing from prior cycle peaks above 2,000 thousand units, signaling severe demand destruction in the housing sector. This housing collapse transmits to broader economy through wealth effects and credit contraction, reducing long-term growth expectations and driving 10-year Treasury yields downward as investors price in recession risk.
candidate_trigger: HOUST falls below 1,500 thousand units or declines more than 10% month-over-month
evidence_refs: fact:Housing starts collapsed from 2.3 million annualized in 2006 to 1.5 million in April 2008
failure_condition: 10-year Treasury yields rise by more than 20 basis points over 20-day window despite HOUST falling below 1,500 thousand, or housing collapse does not precede yield declines within 15 trading days in more than 50% of triggered instances.
